results.query: How do you serve content from a private S3 bucket through CloudFront and prevent direct access to the bucket?

retrieved_chunks.content: CloudFront supports several origin types: an S3 bucket (using either the REST endpoint or the website endpoint), a custom HTTP/HTTPS origin (which can be an ALB, NLB, EC2 instance, API Gateway, MediaPackage, or any internet-reachable HTTP server), and a CloudFront origin group for failover between two origins. To restrict a private S3 bucket so it is only reachable through a specific CloudFront distribution, use Origin Access Control (OAC). OAC is the modern replacement for Origin Access Identity (OAI) and supports SigV4 signing, all S3 regions including those launched after 2022, KMS-encrypted objects, and dynamic content methods like PUT and DELETE. With OAC enabled on the origin, CloudFront signs every request to S3 with the distribution's identity, and the bucket policy can be written to allow s3:GetObject only when aws:SourceArn equals the distribution's ARN and Service is cloudfront.amazonaws.com. The bucket itself can stay private with Block Public Access on. For custom origins, CloudFront supports custom headers (sent on every origin request, often used to validate the request originated from CloudFront and not directly from the internet), origin protocol policy (HTTP only, HTTPS only, or match-viewer), and connection timeout / read timeout / keep-alive timeout settings.

CloudFront supports two ways to grant time-limited access to private content: signed URLs and signed cookies. Both rely on a CloudFront key group containing one or more public keys; the corresponding private key is held by a trusted signer who creates the signature. A signed URL embeds the policy and signature in the URL itself as query parameters and is therefore tied to a single object (or a narrow URL pattern with a custom policy). Signed cookies set CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id cookies on the viewer's browser; subsequent requests within the same domain automatically include the cookies, allowing access to many objects without changing the URL of each one. Use signed URLs when the client cannot accept cookies (e.g., a media player or a download link), or when access is for a single object. Use signed cookies when distributing access to many files such as paginated content, an HLS playlist with many segments, or an authenticated subdirectory of a website. Both support a 'canned policy' (one URL, one expiration) and a 'custom policy' (multiple URLs or path patterns, IP range restrictions, start time as well as expiration). Trusted signers must be configured per cache behavior, and the cache behavior must have 'Restrict viewer access' enabled.

Amazon CloudFront is AWS's content delivery network (CDN) that distributes content from a global network of edge locations to reduce latency for end users. A CloudFront distribution is the top-level resource: it has one or more origins (where the content lives), a set of cache behaviors (rules for how to handle requests matching specific URL patterns), and a globally accessible domain name like d1234abcd.cloudfront.net. Requests from viewers terminate at the nearest edge location, where TLS is negotiated; if the requested object is in the edge cache it is served immediately, otherwise CloudFront fetches it from the origin (often through a regional edge cache that fronts the origin to amplify cache hit rate). CloudFront supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Distributions can serve static assets, dynamic content, video streaming (HLS/DASH), and even APIs. Pricing is based on data transferred out to viewers, number of HTTP/HTTPS requests, and any add-on features like field-level encryption or function executions. CloudFront integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free public TLS certificates, AWS WAF for layer 7 protection, AWS Shield for DDoS protection (Standard included free, Advanced as a paid tier), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for request and response manipulation.

CloudFront supports geo-restriction (also called geo-blocking) at the distribution level, letting you allow or block requests based on the viewer's country as inferred from a third-party IP-to-country database. The configuration is an allowlist or blocklist of ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country codes (US, GB, DE, etc.). When a viewer's request originates from a blocked country, CloudFront returns HTTP 403 before the request reaches the origin, and the response is cached so subsequent blocked requests are even cheaper. Geo-restriction is suitable for compliance-driven regional licensing (e.g., video catalog rights) but should not be relied on as a security boundary because IP-to-country mapping is imperfect and easily bypassed with a VPN. For more granular geo controls, AWS WAF can be attached to the distribution and provides geo-match rules combinable with rate limiting, IP sets, and other criteria. WAF also exposes the country in the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header (when explicitly added through a cache or origin request policy), which Lambda@Edge or origin code can use to apply business logic such as showing localized content rather than blocking outright.

Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) is an object storage service that stores data as objects within buckets. Each bucket has a globally unique name across all AWS accounts and is created in a specific AWS Region. Objects consist of the data itself, a key (the unique identifier within the bucket), version ID, and metadata. S3 provides 99.999999999% (11 nines) of data durability by redundantly storing objects across multiple devices in multiple Availability Zones within a Region. The service is designed for 99.99% availability over a given year for the S3 Standard storage class. Access to buckets and objects is denied by default; permissions are granted explicitly through IAM policies, bucket policies, ACLs, or pre-signed URLs. S3 supports both virtual-hosted-style URLs (bucket.s3.region.amazonaws.com) and path-style URLs (s3.region.amazonaws.com/bucket), though AWS has deprecated path-style for new buckets. Common interaction patterns include the AWS CLI, SDKs in every major language, the REST API, and the S3 console. S3 is the foundation of many data lake architectures and is integrated with practically every other AWS service for data input and output.

results.answer:
Based on the retrieved documentation, here is how to serve content from a private S3 bucket through CloudFront while preventing direct access:

## Use Origin Access Control (OAC)

**OAC** is the modern, recommended approach (replacing the older Origin Access Identity/OAI). Here's how it works:

### Setup Steps

1. **Enable OAC on the CloudFront origin** – This causes CloudFront to sign every request it makes to S3 using **SigV4 signing** with the distribution's identity.

2. **Configure the S3 Bucket Policy** – Write a bucket policy that allows `s3:GetObject` **only when**:
   - `aws:SourceArn` equals the distribution's ARN, **and**
   - The `Service` is `cloudfront.amazonaws.com`

3. **Enable Block Public Access on the S3 bucket** – The bucket itself remains fully private; no direct public access is permitted.

### Why OAC over OAI?

OAC is preferred because it supports:
- **SigV4 signing**
- **All S3 regions**, including those launched after 2022
- **KMS-encrypted objects**
- **Dynamic content methods** like `PUT` and `DELETE`

### Result

With this configuration:
- Viewers can only access bucket content **through the CloudFront distribution**
- Any direct requests to the S3 bucket are **denied** (since the bucket has Block Public Access enabled and the bucket policy restricts access to CloudFront only)
- CloudFront acts as the sole authorized gateway to the private content

results.answer:
# Serving Private S3 Content Through CloudFront

To serve content from a private S3 bucket through CloudFront while preventing direct access, use **Origin Access Control (OAC)**:

## Setup Steps

1. **Enable OAC on the CloudFront Origin**
   - OAC is the modern replacement for Origin Access Identity (OAI)
   - It supports SigV4 signing, all S3 regions (including those launched after 2022), KMS-encrypted objects, and dynamic content methods like PUT and DELETE

2. **Configure Bucket Policy**
   - Write the bucket policy to allow `s3:GetObject` only when:
     - `aws:SourceArn` equals the CloudFront distribution's ARN
     - `Service` is `cloudfront.amazonaws.com`

3. **Keep the Bucket Private**
   - Enable Block Public Access on the S3 bucket itself

## How It Works

With OAC enabled, CloudFront signs every request to S3 using the distribution's identity. This means:
- Only requests from your specific CloudFront distribution are authorized
- Direct access to the bucket (even if someone discovers the bucket name) is denied
- All traffic must route through CloudFront

This approach ensures that your private S3 bucket content is only accessible through your CloudFront distribution, providing a secure content delivery setup.

results.answer:
To serve content from a private S3 bucket through CloudFront and prevent direct access to the bucket, you can use Origin Access Control (OAC). Here’s how to set it up:

1. **Enable Origin Access Control (OAC)**: Use OAC to ensure that the S3 bucket is only accessible through a specific CloudFront distribution. OAC automatically signs every request made to S3 with the distribution's identity.

2. **Configure Bucket Policy**: Write the S3 bucket policy to allow `s3:GetObject` permissions only when the request source matches specific conditions. The bucket policy should check that `aws:SourceArn` equals the CloudFront distribution's ARN and that the Service is `cloudfront.amazonaws.com`.

3. **Keep the Bucket Private**: Ensure that the bucket remains private by enabling Block Public Access settings.

With OAC enabled, CloudFront acts as a mediator for all requests to the S3 bucket, which allows you to control access and prevent public direct access to the content stored in the bucket.

Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) is an object storage service that stores data as objects within buckets. Each bucket has a globally unique name across all AWS accounts and is created in a specific AWS Region. Objects consist of the data itself, a key (the unique identifier within the bucket), version ID, and metadata. S3 provides 99.999999999% (11 nines) of data durability by redundantly storing objects across multiple devices in multiple Availability Zones within a Region. The service is designed for 99.99% availability over a given year for the S3 Standard storage class. Access to buckets and objects is denied by default; permissions are granted explicitly through IAM policies, bucket policies, ACLs, or pre-signed URLs. S3 supports both virtual-hosted-style URLs (bucket.s3.region.amazonaws.com) and path-style URLs (s3.region.amazonaws.com/bucket), though AWS has deprecated path-style for new buckets. Common interaction patterns include the AWS CLI, SDKs in every major language, the REST API, and the S3 console. S3 is the foundation of many data lake architectures and is integrated with practically every other AWS service for data input and output.

To serve content from a private S3 bucket through CloudFront and prevent direct access to the bucket, you should use **Origin Access Control (OAC)**. OAC is the modern replacement for Origin Access Identity (OAI) and offers several enhancements like SigV4 signing, support for all S3 regions (including those launched after 2022), KMS-encrypted objects, and dynamic content methods like PUT and DELETE.

With OAC enabled on the S3 origin, CloudFront signs every request to S3 using the distribution's identity. You can then configure your S3 bucket policy to only allow `s3:GetObject` requests when the `aws:SourceArn` equals the CloudFront distribution's ARN and the `Service` is `cloudfront.amazonaws.com`. This ensures that content is only reachable through the specified CloudFront distribution. The S3 bucket itself can remain private with Block Public Access enabled.

results.query: How do you serve content from a private S3 bucket through CloudFront and prevent direct access to the bucket?

retrieved_chunks.content: CloudFront supports several origin types: an S3 bucket (using either the REST endpoint or the website endpoint), a custom HTTP/HTTPS origin (which can be an ALB, NLB, EC2 instance, API Gateway, MediaPackage, or any internet-reachable HTTP server), and a CloudFront origin group for failover between two origins. To restrict a private S3 bucket so it is only reachable through a specific CloudFront distribution, use Origin Access Control (OAC). OAC is the modern replacement for Origin Access Identity (OAI) and supports SigV4 signing, all S3 regions including those launched after 2022, KMS-encrypted objects, and dynamic content methods like PUT and DELETE. With OAC enabled on the origin, CloudFront signs every request to S3 with the distribution's identity, and the bucket policy can be written to allow s3:GetObject only when aws:SourceArn equals the distribution's ARN and Service is cloudfront.amazonaws.com. The bucket itself can stay private with Block Public Access on. For custom origins, CloudFront supports custom headers (sent on every origin request, often used to validate the request originated from CloudFront and not directly from the internet), origin protocol policy (HTTP only, HTTPS only, or match-viewer), and connection timeout / read timeout / keep-alive timeout settings.

CloudFront supports two ways to grant time-limited access to private content: signed URLs and signed cookies. Both rely on a CloudFront key group containing one or more public keys; the corresponding private key is held by a trusted signer who creates the signature. A signed URL embeds the policy and signature in the URL itself as query parameters and is therefore tied to a single object (or a narrow URL pattern with a custom policy). Signed cookies set CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id cookies on the viewer's browser; subsequent requests within the same domain automatically include the cookies, allowing access to many objects without changing the URL of each one. Use signed URLs when the client cannot accept cookies (e.g., a media player or a download link), or when access is for a single object. Use signed cookies when distributing access to many files such as paginated content, an HLS playlist with many segments, or an authenticated subdirectory of a website. Both support a 'canned policy' (one URL, one expiration) and a 'custom policy' (multiple URLs or path patterns, IP range restrictions, start time as well as expiration). Trusted signers must be configured per cache behavior, and the cache behavior must have 'Restrict viewer access' enabled.

Amazon CloudFront is AWS's content delivery network (CDN) that distributes content from a global network of edge locations to reduce latency for end users. A CloudFront distribution is the top-level resource: it has one or more origins (where the content lives), a set of cache behaviors (rules for how to handle requests matching specific URL patterns), and a globally accessible domain name like d1234abcd.cloudfront.net. Requests from viewers terminate at the nearest edge location, where TLS is negotiated; if the requested object is in the edge cache it is served immediately, otherwise CloudFront fetches it from the origin (often through a regional edge cache that fronts the origin to amplify cache hit rate). CloudFront supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Distributions can serve static assets, dynamic content, video streaming (HLS/DASH), and even APIs. Pricing is based on data transferred out to viewers, number of HTTP/HTTPS requests, and any add-on features like field-level encryption or function executions. CloudFront integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free public TLS certificates, AWS WAF for layer 7 protection, AWS Shield for DDoS protection (Standard included free, Advanced as a paid tier), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for request and response manipulation.

CloudFront supports geo-restriction (also called geo-blocking) at the distribution level, letting you allow or block requests based on the viewer's country as inferred from a third-party IP-to-country database. The configuration is an allowlist or blocklist of ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country codes (US, GB, DE, etc.). When a viewer's request originates from a blocked country, CloudFront returns HTTP 403 before the request reaches the origin, and the response is cached so subsequent blocked requests are even cheaper. Geo-restriction is suitable for compliance-driven regional licensing (e.g., video catalog rights) but should not be relied on as a security boundary because IP-to-country mapping is imperfect and easily bypassed with a VPN. For more granular geo controls, AWS WAF can be attached to the distribution and provides geo-match rules combinable with rate limiting, IP sets, and other criteria. WAF also exposes the country in the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header (when explicitly added through a cache or origin request policy), which Lambda@Edge or origin code can use to apply business logic such as showing localized content rather than blocking outright.

Objects consist of the data itself, a key (the unique identifier within the bucket), version ID, and metadata. S3 provides 99.999999999% (11 nines) of data durability by redundantly storing objects across multiple devices in multiple Availability Zones within a Region. The service is designed for 99.99% availability over a given year for the S3 Standard storage class. Access to buckets and objects is denied by default; permissions are granted explicitly through IAM policies, bucket policies, ACLs, or pre-signed URLs. S3 supports both virtual-hosted-style URLs (bucket.s3.region.amazonaws.com) and path-style URLs (s3.region.amazonaws.com/bucket), though AWS has deprecated path-style for new buckets. Common interaction patterns include the AWS CLI, SDKs in every major language, the REST API, and the S3 console. Here's how it works:
1.  **Enable OAC on the CloudFront distribution's origin**: This allows CloudFront to sign requests to S3 using the distribution's identity [1].
2.  **Update the S3 bucket policy**: Configure the bucket policy to allow `s3:GetObject` requests only when the `aws:SourceArn` matches the CloudFront distribution's ARN and the service is `cloudfront.amazonaws.com` [1].
3.  **Keep the S3 bucket private**: You can maintain "Block Public Access" settings on the S3 bucket itself [1].

This setup ensures that content is only accessible through your CloudFront distribution, even though the S3 bucket remains private [1]. OAC is the modern replacement for Origin Access Identity (OAI) and offers enhanced features like SigV4 signing and support for KMS-encrypted objects [1].

results.query: What is the difference between Cognito User Pools and Identity Pools, and what tokens does a User Pool issue on sign-in?

retrieved_chunks.content: Amazon Cognito provides two distinct services that are often confused: Cognito User Pools and Cognito Identity Pools. A User Pool is a managed user directory that handles sign-up, sign-in, password reset, MFA, and federated identity, and issues OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) once a user authenticates. The User Pool itself is the OIDC identity provider; downstream applications validate the JWTs it issues. An Identity Pool, by contrast, exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, an external IdP like Google or Facebook, or a SAML provider) for short-lived AWS credentials via STS. Identity Pools support both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) identities and are the bridge between an application's user-facing authentication and direct AWS API access from clients. A typical pattern is to authenticate users against a User Pool, get an ID token, and then exchange that token at an Identity Pool for temporary IAM credentials that let the client (e.g., a mobile app) call S3 or DynamoDB directly. Either service can be used independently: User Pools alone for token-based API auth, Identity Pools alone for federated AWS access without a User Pool.

After a successful sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three tokens. The ID token is a JWT that contains user identity claims (sub, email, name, custom attributes, cognito:groups) and is intended to identify the user to the application; it is not meant to authorize API calls. The access token is a JWT that contains scopes (cognito:groups, the OAuth scopes granted, and a token_use claim of 'access') and is meant to be sent on API requests for authorization. The refresh token is an opaque string used to request fresh access and ID tokens from the /oauth2/token endpoint without re-authenticating; refresh tokens have a configurable lifetime from 1 hour to 10 years and can be revoked. Both ID and access tokens have a default 1-hour expiration, configurable per app client between 5 minutes and 24 hours. Both are signed with RS256 using a User Pool-specific key pair. The 'aud' claim of the ID token is the app client ID; the 'iss' claim is https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}; the 'token_use' claim is 'id' or 'access'. APIs validating tokens should always check signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and token_use.

Cognito User Pools implement OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) standards, exposing standard endpoints like /oauth2/authorize, /oauth2/token, /oauth2/userInfo, and /.well-known/openid-configuration plus /.well-known/jwks.json. Applications register one or more app clients on the User Pool, each with its own client ID and optional client secret, allowed OAuth flows (authorization code, implicit, client credentials), allowed scopes (openid, profile, email, custom scopes), and allowed callback / logout URLs. The authorization code flow with PKCE is the recommended flow for browser-based and mobile clients; the implicit flow is deprecated for interactive use. Client credentials flow is used for machine-to-machine integrations where there is no user, and requires a client secret. The token endpoint can return ID tokens (claims about the user, signed JWT), access tokens (signed JWT used for API authorization), and refresh tokens (opaque, used to obtain new access and ID tokens without re-prompting the user). All tokens are signed with RS256, and the public key is published at the JWKS endpoint so APIs can validate signatures offline.

Cognito User Pools support federation with external identity providers in two main flavors. Social federation supports Google, Facebook, Apple, and Amazon out of the box: you register an OAuth app with the provider, add the client ID and secret to the User Pool's identity provider configuration, and choose attribute mappings from the provider's claims to User Pool attributes. Once configured, the Hosted UI displays a sign-in-with-{provider} button, and on first login Cognito creates a federated user in the User Pool linked to the external identity. SAML federation supports any SAML 2.0 IdP (Okta, Azure AD, ADFS, Auth0, etc.): you upload the IdP metadata XML or URL, configure attribute mappings, and Cognito acts as the SAML SP. OIDC federation supports any OIDC-compliant IdP: you supply the issuer URL, client ID, secret, and authorized scopes. Federated users are created lazily on first sign-in and have a 'sub' that is unique within the User Pool; the original IdP identity is recorded in the user's identities attribute. Multiple IdPs can be enabled simultaneously; the Hosted UI shows one button per enabled provider plus the standard username/password form if local sign-in is allowed.

To validate a Cognito-issued JWT, an API server fetches the JSON Web Key Set (JWKS) from the User Pool's discovery endpoint at https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}/.well-known/jwks.json. The JWKS contains the public keys (RSA modulus and exponent) used to sign tokens, identified by 'kid' (key ID). The validation steps are: parse the JWT header to find the kid, look up the matching key in the JWKS (caching the JWKS in memory and refreshing on a kid miss), verify the RS256 signature, then validate claims: 'iss' must equal the User Pool issuer URL, 'exp' must be in the future, 'token_use' must match the expected token type (e.g., 'access' for API auth), and 'aud' (for ID tokens) or 'client_id' (for access tokens) must match an allowed app client. Cognito does not currently support full token revocation for the access and ID tokens (they are valid until expiry); only the refresh token can be revoked, which prevents new access tokens from being issued. Many libraries (aws-jwt-verify, jose, python-jose, PyJWT) implement these checks; rolling your own validator is an easy place to introduce auth bugs.

results.answer:
## Cognito User Pools vs. Identity Pools

### User Pools
A **User Pool** is a **managed user directory** that handles:
- Sign-up, sign-in, password reset, and MFA
- Federated identity (social, SAML, OIDC providers)
- Issuing **OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens** upon successful authentication

The User Pool itself acts as the **OIDC identity provider**, and downstream applications validate the JWTs it issues.

### Identity Pools
An **Identity Pool** is a bridge between user-facing authentication and **direct AWS API access**. It:
- Exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, Google, Facebook, a SAML provider, etc.) for **short-lived AWS credentials via STS**
- Supports both **authenticated and unauthenticated (guest)** identities
- Does **not** manage users itself — it federates credentials

### Typical Combined Pattern
Authenticate a user against a **User Pool** → receive an ID token → exchange it at an **Identity Pool** → get temporary IAM credentials → call AWS services (e.g., S3, DynamoDB) directly from the client app.

> Either service can be used **independently**: User Pools alone for token-based API auth, Identity Pools alone for federated AWS access without a User Pool.

---

## Tokens Issued by a User Pool on Sign-In

A successful sign-in issues **three tokens**:

| Token | Type | Purpose | Expiration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ID Token** | Signed JWT (RS256) | Contains user identity claims (`sub`, `email`, `name`, custom attributes, `cognito:groups`). Identifies the user to the application. **Not** meant to authorize API calls. | Default 1 hour (configurable: 5 min – 24 hrs) |
| **Access Token** | Signed JWT (RS256) | Contains scopes and a `token_use` claim of `"access"`. **Sent on API requests for authorization.** | Default 1 hour (configurable: 5 min – 24 hrs) |
| **Refresh Token** | Opaque string | Used to request fresh access and ID tokens from the `/oauth2/token` endpoint **without re-authenticating**. Can be revoked. | Configurable: 1 hour – 10 years |

### Key JWT Claims to Validate
- **`iss`**: Must equal `https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}`
- **`aud`**: App client ID (on ID tokens)
- **`token_use`**: `"id"` or `"access"`
- **`exp`**: Must be in the future
- **Signature**: Verified against the User Pool's public keys at the JWKS endpoint

results.answer:
# Cognito User Pools vs Identity Pools

## Key Differences

**Cognito User Pools:**
- A managed user directory that handles authentication (sign-up, sign-in, password reset, MFA, federated identity)
- Issues OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) after a user authenticates
- Acts as the OIDC identity provider itself
- Focused on user authentication and token-based API authorization

**Cognito Identity Pools:**
- Exchanges authenticated identity tokens for short-lived AWS credentials via STS
- Accepts tokens from User Pools, external IdPs (Google, Facebook), or SAML providers
- Supports both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) identities
- Acts as the bridge between user-facing authentication and direct AWS API access

## Typical Usage Pattern
A common pattern is to authenticate users against a User Pool, obtain an ID token, then exchange that token at an Identity Pool for temporary IAM credentials, allowing clients (like mobile apps) to call AWS services like S3 or DynamoDB directly.

## Tokens Issued by User Pools on Sign-In

A Cognito User Pool issues **three tokens**:

1. **ID Token**: A JWT containing user identity claims (sub, email, name, custom attributes, cognito:groups). It identifies the user to the application but is not meant to authorize API calls.

2. **Access Token**: A JWT containing scopes and authorization information. It is meant to be sent on API requests for authorization.

3. **Refresh Token**: An opaque string used to request fresh access and ID tokens without re-authenticating. It has a configurable lifetime from 1 hour to 10 years and can be revoked.

Both ID and access tokens have a default 1-hour expiration (configurable between 5 minutes and 24 hours) and are signed with RS256.

results.answer:
Cognito User Pools and Identity Pools serve different purposes in Amazon Cognito:

- **Cognito User Pools**: This is a managed user directory that handles user sign-up, sign-in, password reset, multi-factor authentication (MFA), and federated identity. It issues OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) once a user successfully authenticates. The User Pool itself acts as the OIDC identity provider, and downstream applications validate the JWTs it issues.

- **Cognito Identity Pools**: In contrast, an Identity Pool exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, or external identity providers like Google or Facebook) for short-lived AWS credentials via the AWS Security Token Service (STS). Identity Pools support both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) identities, enabling the application to allow direct AWS API access from clients.

On sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three tokens:
1. **ID Token**: A JWT containing user identity claims meant for identifying the user to the application (not for authorizing API calls).
2. **Access Token**: A JWT meant to be sent on API requests for authorization, containing scopes and claims relevant for access control.
3. **Refresh Token**: An opaque string used to obtain fresh access and ID tokens from the token endpoint without re-authenticating the user.

results.query: What is the difference between Cognito User Pools and Identity Pools, and what tokens does a User Pool issue on sign-in?

retrieved_chunks.content: Amazon Cognito provides two distinct services that are often confused: Cognito User Pools and Cognito Identity Pools. A User Pool is a managed user directory that handles sign-up, sign-in, password reset, MFA, and federated identity, and issues OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) once a user authenticates. The User Pool itself is the OIDC identity provider; downstream applications validate the JWTs it issues. An Identity Pool, by contrast, exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, an external IdP like Google or Facebook, or a SAML provider) for short-lived AWS credentials via STS. Identity Pools support both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) identities and are the bridge between an application's user-facing authentication and direct AWS API access from clients. A typical pattern is to authenticate users against a User Pool, get an ID token, and then exchange that token at an Identity Pool for temporary IAM credentials that let the client (e.g., a mobile app) call S3 or DynamoDB directly. Either service can be used independently: User Pools alone for token-based API auth, Identity Pools alone for federated AWS access without a User Pool.

After a successful sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three tokens. The ID token is a JWT that contains user identity claims (sub, email, name, custom attributes, cognito:groups) and is intended to identify the user to the application; it is not meant to authorize API calls. The access token is a JWT that contains scopes (cognito:groups, the OAuth scopes granted, and a token_use claim of 'access') and is meant to be sent on API requests for authorization. The refresh token is an opaque string used to request fresh access and ID tokens from the /oauth2/token endpoint without re-authenticating; refresh tokens have a configurable lifetime from 1 hour to 10 years and can be revoked. Both ID and access tokens have a default 1-hour expiration, configurable per app client between 5 minutes and 24 hours. Both are signed with RS256 using a User Pool-specific key pair. The 'aud' claim of the ID token is the app client ID; the 'iss' claim is https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}; the 'token_use' claim is 'id' or 'access'. APIs validating tokens should always check signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and token_use.

Cognito User Pools implement OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) standards, exposing standard endpoints like /oauth2/authorize, /oauth2/token, /oauth2/userInfo, and /.well-known/openid-configuration plus /.well-known/jwks.json. Applications register one or more app clients on the User Pool, each with its own client ID and optional client secret, allowed OAuth flows (authorization code, implicit, client credentials), allowed scopes (openid, profile, email, custom scopes), and allowed callback / logout URLs. The authorization code flow with PKCE is the recommended flow for browser-based and mobile clients; the implicit flow is deprecated for interactive use. Client credentials flow is used for machine-to-machine integrations where there is no user, and requires a client secret. The token endpoint can return ID tokens (claims about the user, signed JWT), access tokens (signed JWT used for API authorization), and refresh tokens (opaque, used to obtain new access and ID tokens without re-prompting the user). All tokens are signed with RS256, and the public key is published at the JWKS endpoint so APIs can validate signatures offline.

Cognito User Pools support federation with external identity providers in two main flavors. Social federation supports Google, Facebook, Apple, and Amazon out of the box: you register an OAuth app with the provider, add the client ID and secret to the User Pool's identity provider configuration, and choose attribute mappings from the provider's claims to User Pool attributes. Once configured, the Hosted UI displays a sign-in-with-{provider} button, and on first login Cognito creates a federated user in the User Pool linked to the external identity. SAML federation supports any SAML 2.0 IdP (Okta, Azure AD, ADFS, Auth0, etc.): you upload the IdP metadata XML or URL, configure attribute mappings, and Cognito acts as the SAML SP. OIDC federation supports any OIDC-compliant IdP: you supply the issuer URL, client ID, secret, and authorized scopes. Federated users are created lazily on first sign-in and have a 'sub' that is unique within the User Pool; the original IdP identity is recorded in the user's identities attribute. Multiple IdPs can be enabled simultaneously; the Hosted UI shows one button per enabled provider plus the standard username/password form if local sign-in is allowed.

To validate a Cognito-issued JWT, an API server fetches the JSON Web Key Set (JWKS) from the User Pool's discovery endpoint at https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}/.well-known/jwks.json. The JWKS contains the public keys (RSA modulus and exponent) used to sign tokens, identified by 'kid' (key ID). The validation steps are: parse the JWT header to find the kid, look up the matching key in the JWKS (caching the JWKS in memory and refreshing on a kid miss), verify the RS256 signature, then validate claims: 'iss' must equal the User Pool issuer URL, 'exp' must be in the future, 'token_use' must match the expected token type (e.g., 'access' for API auth), and 'aud' (for ID tokens) or 'client_id' (for access tokens) must match an allowed app client. Cognito does not currently support full token revocation for the access and ID tokens (they are valid until expiry); only the refresh token can be revoked, which prevents new access tokens from being issued. Many libraries (aws-jwt-verify, jose, python-jose, PyJWT) implement these checks; rolling your own validator is an easy place to introduce auth bugs.

Cognito User Pools and Identity Pools are two distinct services. A User Pool is a managed user directory that handles user sign-up, sign-in, multi-factor authentication, and federated identity, and it acts as an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider, issuing OAuth 2.0/OIDC tokens upon successful user authentication. In contrast, an Identity Pool exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, external IdPs like Google or Facebook, or SAML providers) for short-lived AWS credentials via AWS Security Token Service (STS). Identity Pools support both authenticated and unauthenticated identities and facilitate direct AWS API access from client applications. A common use case is to authenticate users with a User Pool to obtain an ID token, which is then exchanged at an Identity Pool for temporary IAM credentials, enabling the client to interact directly with AWS services like S3 or DynamoDB. Both services can be used independently; User Pools for token-based API authentication, and Identity Pools for federated AWS access.

Upon a successful sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three types of tokens:
1.  **ID token**: This is a JSON Web Token (JWT) containing user identity claims such as `sub`, `email`, `name`, custom attributes, and `cognito:groups`. It is used to identify the user to the application and is not intended for API authorization.
2.  **Access token**: This is also a JWT that contains `scopes` (e.g., `cognito:groups`, OAuth scopes granted, and a `token_use` claim of 'access'). It is specifically designed to be sent with API requests for authorization purposes.
3.  **Refresh token**: This is an opaque string used to request new access and ID tokens from the `/oauth2/token` endpoint without requiring the user to re-authenticate. Refresh tokens have a configurable lifetime ranging from 1 hour to 10 years and can be revoked.

Both ID and access tokens have a default expiration of 1 hour, which can be configured per app client between 5 minutes and 24 hours. They are signed with RS256 using a User Pool-specific key pair.

results.query: How do CloudFront cache behaviors decide what to cache and how do you invalidate content already in the cache?

retrieved_chunks.content: CloudFront cache behaviors define how the distribution handles requests matching specific path patterns. A distribution always has a default cache behavior (path pattern *), and additional behaviors can be added with patterns like /api/*, /static/*, /*.jpg. Behaviors are matched in priority order, with the default behavior used as a fallback. Each behavior specifies the origin, allowed HTTP methods, viewer protocol policy (HTTP, HTTPS, redirect HTTP to HTTPS), cache policy, origin request policy, response headers policy, and any function or Lambda@Edge associations. Cache policies define what is included in the cache key (which headers, cookies, query strings affect caching), what TTLs apply (minimum, default, maximum), and whether to enable Gzip / Brotli compression. The TTL can also be controlled by Cache-Control or Expires headers from the origin: by default CloudFront respects them, capped by the maximum TTL. Setting Cache-Control: no-cache or no-store on the origin response prevents caching at the edge. Cache invalidation is available via the CreateInvalidation API, which removes paths from the cache; the first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free, additional paths cost a small per-path fee. Versioned filenames (e.g., app.v123.js) avoid the need for invalidation by changing the cache key naturally on each release.

Amazon CloudFront is AWS's content delivery network (CDN) that distributes content from a global network of edge locations to reduce latency for end users. A CloudFront distribution is the top-level resource: it has one or more origins (where the content lives), a set of cache behaviors (rules for how to handle requests matching specific URL patterns), and a globally accessible domain name like d1234abcd.cloudfront.net. Requests from viewers terminate at the nearest edge location, where TLS is negotiated; if the requested object is in the edge cache it is served immediately, otherwise CloudFront fetches it from the origin (often through a regional edge cache that fronts the origin to amplify cache hit rate). CloudFront supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Distributions can serve static assets, dynamic content, video streaming (HLS/DASH), and even APIs. Pricing is based on data transferred out to viewers, number of HTTP/HTTPS requests, and any add-on features like field-level encryption or function executions. CloudFront integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free public TLS certificates, AWS WAF for layer 7 protection, AWS Shield for DDoS protection (Standard included free, Advanced as a paid tier), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for request and response manipulation.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

CloudFront supports two ways to grant time-limited access to private content: signed URLs and signed cookies. Both rely on a CloudFront key group containing one or more public keys; the corresponding private key is held by a trusted signer who creates the signature. A signed URL embeds the policy and signature in the URL itself as query parameters and is therefore tied to a single object (or a narrow URL pattern with a custom policy). Signed cookies set CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id cookies on the viewer's browser; subsequent requests within the same domain automatically include the cookies, allowing access to many objects without changing the URL of each one. Use signed URLs when the client cannot accept cookies (e.g., a media player or a download link), or when access is for a single object. Use signed cookies when distributing access to many files such as paginated content, an HLS playlist with many segments, or an authenticated subdirectory of a website. Both support a 'canned policy' (one URL, one expiration) and a 'custom policy' (multiple URLs or path patterns, IP range restrictions, start time as well as expiration). Trusted signers must be configured per cache behavior, and the cache behavior must have 'Restrict viewer access' enabled.

Common Lambda@Edge patterns include authentication, A/B testing, header rewrites, and dynamic content selection. For authentication, a viewer-request function can validate a JWT in a cookie or Authorization header against a JWKS, redirect unauthenticated users to a login page (often a Cognito Hosted UI), and forward authenticated requests to the origin with a normalized identity header. For A/B testing, a viewer-request function reads or sets a sticky cookie indicating the experiment bucket, then rewrites the request path or chooses an origin per bucket; the cookie value can also be added to the cache key so each variant is cached separately. For header rewrites, an origin-response function adds security headers (HSTS, CSP), strips sensitive headers, or normalizes Cache-Control to drive CDN behavior. For dynamic content selection, an origin-request function inspects the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header and rewrites the origin or path so users in different countries see different content while sharing a single distribution. Each pattern should be benchmarked because Lambda@Edge cost compounds quickly under high request volume; CloudFront Functions are often a cheaper alternative for simple viewer-side work.

results.answer:
## CloudFront Cache Behaviors: Caching Decisions & Invalidation

### What Gets Cached (Cache Key)

Cache behaviors use **cache policies** to determine what is included in the cache key — the combination of request attributes that CloudFront uses to identify a unique cached object. The cache key can include:

- **Headers** – specific request headers
- **Cookies** – specific cookie values
- **Query strings** – specific or all query string parameters

Only requests with identical cache key values will be served the same cached object. This means you can fine-tune caching granularity by including or excluding these elements.

---

### How Long Content Is Cached (TTLs)

Cache policies also define **Time-to-Live (TTL)** settings:

- **Minimum TTL**
- **Default TTL**
- **Maximum TTL**

CloudFront respects **`Cache-Control`** or **`Expires`** headers sent by the origin, but caps them at the configured maximum TTL. To **prevent caching entirely**, the origin can send:
- `Cache-Control: no-cache`
- `Cache-Control: no-store`

---

### Invalidating Cached Content

There are two main approaches:

1. **Cache Invalidation API** – Use the `CreateInvalidation` API to explicitly remove specific paths from the edge cache.
   - The **first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free**; additional paths incur a small per-path fee.

2. **Versioned Filenames** *(preferred)* – Change the filename on each release (e.g., `app.v123.js` → `app.v124.js`). This naturally changes the cache key, so CloudFront fetches the new file without requiring any invalidation — and it's free.

> Versioned filenames are generally the recommended approach as they avoid invalidation costs and complexity.

results.answer:
# CloudFront Cache Behaviors and Cache Invalidation

## How Cache Behaviors Decide What to Cache

Cache behaviors use **cache policies** to determine what gets cached:

1. **Cache Key Definition**: The cache policy specifies which request elements are included in the cache key:
   - Headers
   - Cookies
   - Query strings
   
   These elements determine whether a request hits a cached object or requires a new fetch from the origin.

2. **TTL (Time-to-Live) Settings**: Cache policies define:
   - Minimum TTL
   - Default TTL
   - Maximum TTL

3. **Origin Control**: CloudFront respects `Cache-Control` and `Expires` headers from the origin response, but caps them by the maximum TTL. Setting `Cache-Control: no-cache` or `no-store` on the origin response prevents caching at the edge entirely.

4. **Compression**: Cache policies can enable Gzip and Brotli compression.

## Cache Invalidation

You can invalidate (remove) cached content using the **CreateInvalidation API**:

- **Free Tier**: The first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free
- **Additional Paths**: Additional paths incur a small per-path fee

### Alternative: Versioned Filenames

Rather than invalidating, a best practice is to use **versioned filenames** (e.g., `app.v123.js`). This naturally changes the cache key on each release, avoiding invalidation costs and complexity entirely.

A CloudFront distribution is the top-level resource: it has one or more origins (where the content lives), a set of cache behaviors (rules for how to handle requests matching specific URL patterns), and a globally accessible domain name like d1234abcd.cloudfront.net. Requests from viewers terminate at the nearest edge location, where TLS is negotiated; if the requested object is in the edge cache it is served immediately, otherwise CloudFront fetches it from the origin (often through a regional edge cache that fronts the origin to amplify cache hit rate). CloudFront supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Distributions can serve static assets, dynamic content, video streaming (HLS/DASH), and even APIs. Pricing is based on data transferred out to viewers, number of HTTP/HTTPS requests, and any add-on features like field-level encryption or function executions. CloudFront integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free public TLS certificates, AWS WAF for layer 7 protection, AWS Shield for DDoS protection (Standard included free, Advanced as a paid tier), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for request and response manipulation.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

CloudFront supports two ways to grant time-limited access to private content: signed URLs and signed cookies. Both rely on a CloudFront key group containing one or more public keys; the corresponding private key is held by a trusted signer who creates the signature. A signed URL embeds the policy and signature in the URL itself as query parameters and is therefore tied to a single object (or a narrow URL pattern with a custom policy). Signed cookies set CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id cookies on the viewer's browser; subsequent requests within the same domain automatically include the cookies, allowing access to many objects without changing the URL of each one. Use signed URLs when the client cannot accept cookies (e.g., a media player or a download link), or when access is for a single object. Use signed cookies when distributing access to many files such as paginated content, an HLS playlist with many segments, or an authenticated subdirectory of a website. Both support a 'canned policy' (one URL, one expiration) and a 'custom policy' (multiple URLs or path patterns, IP range restrictions, start time as well as expiration). Trusted signers must be configured per cache behavior, and the cache behavior must have 'Restrict viewer access' enabled.

Common Lambda@Edge patterns include authentication, A/B testing, header rewrites, and dynamic content selection. For authentication, a viewer-request function can validate a JWT in a cookie or Authorization header against a JWKS, redirect unauthenticated users to a login page (often a Cognito Hosted UI), and forward authenticated requests to the origin with a normalized identity header. For A/B testing, a viewer-request function reads or sets a sticky cookie indicating the experiment bucket, then rewrites the request path or chooses an origin per bucket; the cookie value can also be added to the cache key so each variant is cached separately. For header rewrites, an origin-response function adds security headers (HSTS, CSP), strips sensitive headers, or normalizes Cache-Control to drive CDN behavior. For dynamic content selection, an origin-request function inspects the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header and rewrites the origin or path so users in different countries see different content while sharing a single distribution. Each pattern should be benchmarked because Lambda@Edge cost compounds quickly under high request volume; CloudFront Functions are often a cheaper alternative for simple viewer-side work.

results.answer:
CloudFront cache behaviors decide what to cache based on several factors specified within each behavior. Each behavior includes definitions for the origin, allowed HTTP methods, viewer protocol policy, cache policy, origin request policy, response headers policy, and any function or Lambda@Edge associations. 

Cache policies define what is included in the cache key, determining how headers, cookies, and query strings affect caching. They also specify the Time-To-Live (TTL) values (minimum, default, maximum) for cached objects, and whether Gzip or Brotli compression is enabled. By default, CloudFront respects Cache-Control or Expires headers from the origin, capped by the maximum TTL. Setting Cache-Control: no-cache or no-store on the origin response can prevent caching at the edge.

To invalidate content already in the cache, you can use the CreateInvalidation API, which removes specified paths from the cache. The first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free, while additional paths incur a per-path fee.

results.answer:
CloudFront cache behaviors determine what is cached through their associated **cache policy**. This policy defines:
*   What elements are included in the **cache key** (e.g., headers, cookies, query strings that affect caching).
*   The **time-to-live (TTL)** settings, including minimum, default, and maximum durations.
*   Whether **Gzip / Brotli compression** is enabled.

CloudFront also respects `Cache-Control` or `Expires` headers from the origin, capped by the maximum TTL defined in the cache policy. If an origin response includes `Cache-Control: no-cache` or `no-store`, the content will not be cached at the edge.

To invalidate content already in the cache, you can use the **CreateInvalidation API**. This removes specified paths from the cache. Alternatively, using **versioned filenames** (e.g., `app.v123.js`) avoids the need for explicit invalidation, as a new filename naturally creates a new cache key on each release.

results.answer:
CloudFront cache behaviors determine what to cache by defining rules for specific URL patterns. These rules specify the origin, allowed HTTP methods, viewer protocol policy, cache policy, origin request policy, response headers policy, and any associated functions. The **cache policy** is particularly important as it defines what is included in the cache key (headers, cookies, query strings), applicable TTLs (minimum, default, maximum), and compression settings. CloudFront also respects `Cache-Control` or `Expires` headers from the origin, capped by the maximum TTL, and prevents caching if `Cache-Control: no-cache` or `no-store` is set on the origin response.

To invalidate content already in the cache, you can use the **`CreateInvalidation` API**. This API removes specified paths from the cache. The first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free, with a small per-path fee for additional paths. An alternative to invalidation is using versioned filenames (e.g., `app.v123.js`), which naturally change the cache key with each release and avoid the need for manual invalidation.

results.query: Explain the four Lambda@Edge event types and the practical effects of replication on debugging.

retrieved_chunks.content: Lambda@Edge supports four CloudFront event types, each fired at a different point in the request lifecycle. viewer-request runs on every request from a viewer before the cache is consulted, so it sees every request and is the right place for redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that should affect cache key. origin-request runs only on cache misses, just before CloudFront forwards to the origin; this is the place to rewrite the URL path or change the origin. origin-response runs after the origin returns a response and before CloudFront caches it; this is where to add or rewrite headers that should be cached, or to handle origin errors with custom responses. viewer-response runs on every response sent to the viewer, including cache hits, so it is the right place for headers that must be present on every response (though CloudFront response headers policies are usually a cheaper option). Each event type has different size limits (the viewer-side caps the request body at 40 KB, origin-side at 1 MB) and different latency budgets (viewer-side max 5 seconds, origin-side max 30 seconds). Choosing the right event is critical: putting expensive logic on viewer-request multiplies cost compared to putting it on origin-request.

Lambda@Edge integrates AWS Lambda with CloudFront, letting you run Node.js or Python functions in response to CloudFront events. Functions are authored and uploaded as ordinary Lambda functions in the us-east-1 region, then 'published' to a numbered version and associated with a specific cache behavior on a distribution. CloudFront automatically replicates the function to all regional edge caches; replication completes within a few minutes after publishing. Functions can be associated to four event types: viewer-request, origin-request, origin-response, and viewer-response. Lambda@Edge functions execute in the closest AWS region to the viewer (not literally at every edge POP), and they have access to the full Node.js / Python runtime environment and a limited set of npm or pip packages bundled into the deployment. The maximum execution time is 5 seconds for viewer-facing events and 30 seconds for origin events; memory is configurable up to 10 GB. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second of compute, separately from regular Lambda. Common limitations: no environment variables, no VPC access, request body size limits (40 KB for viewer events, 1 MB for origin events), and a smaller set of supported runtimes than regular Lambda.

Lambda@Edge is a feature of Amazon CloudFront that runs AWS Lambda functions at CloudFront points of presence in response to viewer or origin events. Functions are written in Node.js or Python, packaged like ordinary Lambda functions, and must be deployed in the us-east-1 (N. Virginia) region. To use a function with CloudFront, you publish a numbered version (Lambda@Edge does not support $LATEST or aliases) and associate that exact version ARN with a cache behavior on a distribution. CloudFront then replicates the code to all regional edge caches around the world; replication usually completes within a few minutes but can take longer. Each function executes in the AWS region nearest the viewer, not literally at the POP itself. Lambda@Edge can modify the request and response that flows between the viewer, CloudFront, and the origin; it cannot serve traffic without CloudFront. Use cases include header rewrites, A/B testing logic, JWT validation, dynamic origin selection based on geography or device, image resizing on demand, custom auth, and request normalization. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second separately from regular Lambda, and pricing is higher than standard Lambda because of the global replication infrastructure.

CloudFront Functions and Lambda@Edge are two different ways to run code at the CloudFront layer, with very different tradeoffs. CloudFront Functions are lightweight, JavaScript-only (ECMAScript 5.1 plus a subset of newer features) functions that execute in microseconds at the edge POP itself (not a regional edge cache), with a maximum execution time of about 1 ms and 2 MB of memory. They support only viewer-request and viewer-response event types, have no network or filesystem access, and cannot make outbound calls. They cost roughly 1/6 the price of Lambda@Edge per request and are designed for high-volume, low-latency request manipulation: header rewrites, simple URL normalization, redirects, A/B test cookie assignment, JWT validation against a static key, basic authorization checks. Lambda@Edge runs full Node.js or Python with up to 10 GB memory and 5–30 second timeouts, supports outbound network calls (including to other AWS services or third-party APIs), and works on origin-request and origin-response events as well as viewer events. Use CloudFront Functions for hot-path, stateless transformations and Lambda@Edge for anything requiring external lookups, larger code, or origin-side logic.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

results.answer:
## Lambda@Edge Event Types

Based on the retrieved documentation, there are four CloudFront event types, each firing at a distinct point in the request lifecycle:

### 1. **viewer-request**
- Runs on **every request** from a viewer, *before* the cache is consulted
- Sees 100% of incoming traffic (including cache hits)
- Best for: redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that should affect the cache key
- **Limits:** 40 KB request body cap, 5-second max execution time
- ⚠️ Putting expensive logic here multiplies cost significantly compared to using `origin-request`

### 2. **origin-request**
- Runs only on **cache misses**, just before CloudFront forwards the request to the origin
- Best for: rewriting URL paths, changing the origin dynamically, dynamic origin selection based on geography or device
- **Limits:** 1 MB request body cap, 30-second max execution time

### 3. **origin-response**
- Runs after the **origin returns a response**, before CloudFront caches it
- Best for: adding or rewriting headers that should be cached, handling origin errors with custom responses
- **Limits:** 1 MB body cap, 30-second max execution time

### 4. **viewer-response**
- Runs on **every response** sent to the viewer, including cache hits
- Best for: headers that must be present on every response
- Note: CloudFront response headers policies are often a cheaper alternative for simple header injection
- **Limits:** 40 KB body cap, 5-second max execution time

---

## Practical Effects of Replication on Debugging

Replication introduces several significant debugging challenges:

1. **Logs are geographically scattered.** Because a Lambda@Edge function executes in whichever AWS region is *nearest the viewer*, CloudWatch log groups are created in many different regions. A single function can have logs spread across **dozens of regions**, making it difficult to trace a specific request or aggregate log data.

2. **No centralized log search.** While CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for a high-level view, log searches generally require **querying each region individually**, complicating troubleshooting.

3. **Cold starts are unpredictable across regions.** Since provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, any region that hasn't seen traffic in a while may experience a cold start (adding hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js, and potentially more for Python). The only mitigation is maintaining steady traffic across all regions.

4. **Replicated versions are read-only and slow to clean up.** You cannot delete a replicated function version directly. To remove one, you must first dissociate it from all distributions and behaviors, then wait for replicas to be cleaned up — a process that *typically takes a few hours but can take longer* — before the underlying function version can be deleted.

In summary, the global replication model that gives Lambda@Edge its low-latency reach also makes observability and lifecycle management considerably more complex than standard Lambda.

Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

results.answer:
# Lambda@Edge Event Types and Replication's Impact on Debugging

## The Four Event Types

Lambda@Edge supports four CloudFront event types, each firing at a different point in the request lifecycle:

1. **viewer-request**: Runs on every request from a viewer before the cache is consulted. It sees all requests and is ideal for redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that should affect the cache key.

2. **origin-request**: Runs only on cache misses, just before CloudFront forwards to the origin. This is where to rewrite URL paths or change the origin.

3. **origin-response**: Runs after the origin returns a response and before CloudFront caches it. Use this to add or rewrite headers that should be cached, or to handle origin errors with custom responses.

4. **viewer-response**: Runs on every response sent to the viewer, including cache hits. This is the right place for headers that must be present on every response (though CloudFront response headers policies are often cheaper).

### Performance Considerations

Choosing the right event is critical because it affects cost and latency. Each event type has different constraints:
- **Viewer-side events** (viewer-request and viewer-response): max 5 seconds execution time, 40 KB request body limit
- **Origin-side events** (origin-request and origin-response): max 30 seconds execution time, 1 MB request body limit

Putting expensive logic on viewer-request multiplies cost compared to origin-request.

## Replication's Practical Effects on Debugging

When you associate a Lambda@Edge function with CloudFront, it's replicated from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches worldwide. This creates significant debugging challenges:

- **Distributed logs**: CloudWatch log groups exist in many regions, with a single function's logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation.

- **Log search complexity**: Log searches generally require querying each region individually, making it difficult to get a complete picture of function behavior.

- **Partial visibility**: While CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an overview, you cannot easily see all logs in one place.

This distributed nature means troubleshooting requires more effort than traditional Lambda debugging and makes it harder to correlate related invocations across regions.

Lambda@Edge integrates AWS Lambda with CloudFront, letting you run Node.js or Python functions in response to CloudFront events. Functions are authored and uploaded as ordinary Lambda functions in the us-east-1 region, then 'published' to a numbered version and associated with a specific cache behavior on a distribution. CloudFront automatically replicates the function to all regional edge caches; replication completes within a few minutes after publishing. Functions can be associated to four event types: viewer-request, origin-request, origin-response, and viewer-response. Lambda@Edge functions execute in the closest AWS region to the viewer (not literally at every edge POP), and they have access to the full Node.js / Python runtime environment and a limited set of npm or pip packages bundled into the deployment. The maximum execution time is 5 seconds for viewer-facing events and 30 seconds for origin events; memory is configurable up to 10 GB. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second of compute, separately from regular Lambda. Common limitations: no environment variables, no VPC access, request body size limits (40 KB for viewer events, 1 MB for origin events), and a smaller set of supported runtimes than regular Lambda.

Lambda@Edge is a feature of Amazon CloudFront that runs AWS Lambda functions at CloudFront points of presence in response to viewer or origin events. Functions are written in Node.js or Python, packaged like ordinary Lambda functions, and must be deployed in the us-east-1 (N. Virginia) region. To use a function with CloudFront, you publish a numbered version (Lambda@Edge does not support $LATEST or aliases) and associate that exact version ARN with a cache behavior on a distribution. CloudFront then replicates the code to all regional edge caches around the world; replication usually completes within a few minutes but can take longer. Each function executes in the AWS region nearest the viewer, not literally at the POP itself. Lambda@Edge can modify the request and response that flows between the viewer, CloudFront, and the origin; it cannot serve traffic without CloudFront. Use cases include header rewrites, A/B testing logic, JWT validation, dynamic origin selection based on geography or device, image resizing on demand, custom auth, and request normalization. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second separately from regular Lambda, and pricing is higher than standard Lambda because of the global replication infrastructure.

CloudFront Functions and Lambda@Edge are two different ways to run code at the CloudFront layer, with very different tradeoffs. CloudFront Functions are lightweight, JavaScript-only (ECMAScript 5.1 plus a subset of newer features) functions that execute in microseconds at the edge POP itself (not a regional edge cache), with a maximum execution time of about 1 ms and 2 MB of memory. They support only viewer-request and viewer-response event types, have no network or filesystem access, and cannot make outbound calls. They cost roughly 1/6 the price of Lambda@Edge per request and are designed for high-volume, low-latency request manipulation: header rewrites, simple URL normalization, redirects, A/B test cookie assignment, JWT validation against a static key, basic authorization checks. Lambda@Edge runs full Node.js or Python with up to 10 GB memory and 5–30 second timeouts, supports outbound network calls (including to other AWS services or third-party APIs), and works on origin-request and origin-response events as well as viewer events. Use CloudFront Functions for hot-path, stateless transformations and Lambda@Edge for anything requiring external lookups, larger code, or origin-side logic.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

*   **viewer-response:** This event triggers on every response sent to the viewer, including cache hits. It's the right place for headers that must be present on all responses.
